text: Abstract Nanoparticles with well-defined dimensions and functionality at the nanoscale remain a major goal in nanotechnology. Living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) was recently introduced as a method for producing one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) core–shell micellar structures composed of polymeric and molecular amphiphiles through seeded growth. While 1D and 2D structures can be readily formed through the epitaxial growth of a single core-forming polymer or molecular amphiphile, access to complex nanoparticles with segmented cores composed of different crystallizable components that can be used for further functionalization through selective reactions or stimuli-responsiveness remains a significant challenge. Here, we report on the seeded growth approach of living CDSA that can be applied to a wide range of different crystallizable polylactone homopolymer/block copolymer blends to produce 2D platelet micelles with segregated cores. We further demonstrate that the controlled core composition of these 2D structures allows for the spatially selective hydrolytic degradation of the micelles, an example that may be applicable to more complex nanoparticles with multifunctional capabilities for potential use in stimuli-responsive particles for programmed-release and drug-delivery applications.
explanation: The text is highly coherent and uses domain-specific terminology typical of a nanomaterials abstract, which could be written by humans or AI. Given the polished style and plausible technical content, I assign a moderate probability that it is AI-generated.
